data.title: Constellation Energy: Riding Nuclear Demand And The AI Power Boom
data.description:
Constellation Energy (CEG) has retreated from its October 2025 peak as investors dial back their enthusiasm for speculative sectors in tech and clean energy. However, CEG is one of the rare nuclear names that has a tangible business that is projected to compound earnings in the next 3-5 years, supported by AI power demand and favorable policy. The stock has softened against the broader nuclear industry (URA), likely warranting an opportunity to buy.

Key Investment Pillars

1. Massive Nuclear Fleet

According to its SEC filing in February, CEG operates one of the largest nuclear reactor fleets in the U.S., with ~22 GW across 21 units. Together, they produced 183 TWh of zero-emissions electricity in 2025, accounting for 68% of total generation. The fleet achieved a capacity factor of about 94%–96%, setting it as one of the highest in the industry while boosting its margins. Hyperscalers demand uninterruptible, all-weather power generation, to which nuclear has an inherent advantage over wind and solar.

Underpinned by the Inflation Reduction Act's 45U nuclear production tax credit, the fleet enjoyed boosted economics, which helped contribute ~$2.08B of operating revenue in FY2024. The 45U PTC is a price floor, thus preserving upside benefits when markets strengthen.

2. Secular Demand for Power in AI Data Centers

With U.S. electricity demand reaching a record high in 2025, Constellation has situated itself as the primary counterparty for hyperscaler clean energy procurement.

  • Microsoft (MSFT): Constellation has invested $1.6B in the restart of Crane Clean Energy Center, formerly called Three Mile Island. The 20-year PPA for 835 MW has been sold solely to Microsoft and has received approval for a $1B loan guarantee from DOE.
  • Meta (META): Constellation and Meta have signed a 20-year PPA for the output of the Clinton Clean Energy Center beginning in 2027.
  • U.S. Government (GSA): Constellation has signed a 10-year, $840M contract that will supply the U.S. General Services Administration with over 1 million MWH. Along with a separate $172M energy-efficient contract, this is part of a combined $1B energy deal to power federal agencies.
  • CyrusOne: the company's recently signed 380MW PPA with Dallas-based CyrusOne at Freestone Energy Center reinforces Calpine (CEG's subsidiary) as a platform for co-located data centers.

The case has been made by CEO Joe Dominguez, who stated that it is strategic for hyperscalers to locate AI facilities close to large, clean, and reliable power plants.

3. Strategic Re-Rating Through the Calpine Acquisition

One of the most significant near-term catalysts for Constellation has been the $26.6B Calpine acquisition (including $12.7B in assumed net debt) completed in January 2026.

The combined entity will have 55 GW of capacity while serving 2.5 million customers, cementing it as the largest private-sector power producer globally.

Calpine will expand into new geographical markets in the high-demand Texas (ERCOT) and California markets by adding 23 GW of natural gas and geothermal capacity.

The deal is projected to increase adjusted operating EPS by 20% in 2026 by the company. In addition, a minimum of $2/share increase in EPS is expected in the subsequent years. Consequently, this will add over $2B of incremental annual FCF.

The tie-up also allows a credit re-rating for Calpine, as S&P upgraded its credit from 'BB-' to 'BBB+' as a subsidiary, while Constellation's 'BB+' rating remains with a positive outlook.

Overall, we think this is an accretive deal, as it was priced at 7.9x 2026 EV/EBITDA, which is cheaper than the parent's ~20x TTM EV/EBITDA.

Financial Analysis

CEG has a positive earnings history and trajectory from FY2024 to FY2028E. Adjusted EPS grew from $8.67 in FY2024 to $9.39 in FY2025, while the management has guided for $11-$12 in adjusted EPS for FY2026E. This reflects a 22.4% EPS CAGR from FY2025 to FY2028E.

The jump in EBITDA in FY2026E is mainly due to the consolidation of Calpine.

Metric FY2024A FY2025A FY2026E FY2027E FY2028E
Adj. Revenue ($M) 23,568 25,533 31,928 33,639 34,736
Adj. EBITDA ($M) 5,475 4,071 8,810 9,533 11,144
Adj. EPS $8.67 $9.39 $11.61 $13.67 $17.22
EPS YoY Growth +62% +8% +24% +18% +26%

Source: Company, market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates

Disciplined in Capital Allocation

We believe the management has balanced growth well along with returning capital to shareholders. A $5B share repurchase program was raised in the March 2026 outlook call, while dividends have increased 10% in FY2025 and are expected to continue to grow 10% in FY2026E.

The company will spend $3.9B in capital expenditures in FY2026E and is expected due to nuclear uprates, Crane restart, and other expansion.

Key Risks

1. Policy risk: the 45U nuclear PTC is the most significant policy lever for CEG, as about $2.1B in annual revenue depends on this credit. Any revision to this by the administration or adverse IRS guidance on credit eligibility would be a material earnings headwind. Still, we think it is unlikely, as it is clear that the government believes nuclear generation is a strategic investment into the country's future.

2. Calpine integration: CEG has absorbed $12.7B in net debt through the deal and has materially increased its leverage. There is also execution risk with the integration while synergy targets may not be met.

3. Nuclear operating risk: occasionally, nuclear plants endure unplanned and costly outage risks that translate to adverse financial impact.

Verdict and Valuation

Constellation Energy is a prime beneficiary of AI and data center buildout due to their immense power needs. The company is one of the most dominant nuclear players in the U.S., while the Calpine acquisition contributes towards 55 GW in total capacity and $2B in incremental annual FCF. The management has a robust record of beating EPS targets and has secured long-term hyperscaler and federal contracts to provide revenue visibility. Policy support via the 45U PTC makes CEG an even more solid, multi-year earnings compounder.

The stock trades at ~27x forward earnings and ~14x forward EV/EBITDA, which is near their 3-year historical averages. Investors initially reacted negatively to the March 31, 2026, guidance call, as EPS guidance didn't meet analysts's high expectations. But the stock has since fully recovered the losses, potentially hinting that it was an oversold reaction.

We rate CEG a Buy as it can potentially deliver compounding EPS growth in a 3-5 year horizon as it rides on AI power demand and policy support.
